 {"id":"bd-5aad5a9c","title":"Add TestNWayCollision for 5+ clones","description":"## Overview
Add comprehensive tests for N-way (5+) collision resolution to verify the solution scales beyond 3 clones.

## Purpose
While TestThreeCloneCollision validates the basic N-way case, we need to verify:
1. Solution scales to arbitrary N
2. Performance is acceptable with more clones
3. Convergence time is bounded
4. No edge cases in larger collision groups

## Implementation Tasks

### 1. Create TestFiveCloneCollision
File: beads_twoclone_test.go (or new beads_nway_test.go)

```go
func TestFiveCloneCollision(t *testing.T) {
    // Test with 5 clones creating same ID with different content
    // Verify all 5 clones converge after sync rounds
    
    t.Run("SequentialSync", func(t *testing.T) {
        testNCloneCollision(t, 5, "A", "B", "C", "D", "E")
    })
    
    t.Run("ReverseSync", func(t *testing.T) {
        testNCloneCollision(t, 5, "E", "D", "C", "B", "A")
    })
    
    t.Run("RandomSync", func(t *testing.T) {
        testNCloneCollision(t, 5, "C", "A", "E", "B", "D")
    })
}
```

### 2. Implement generalized testNCloneCollision
Generalize the 3-clone test to handle arbitrary N:

```go
func testNCloneCollision(t *testing.T, numClones int, syncOrder ...string) {
    t.Helper()
    
    if len(syncOrder) != numClones {
        t.Fatalf("syncOrder length (%d) must match numClones (%d)", 
            len(syncOrder), numClones)
    }
    
    tmpDir := t.TempDir()
    
    // Setup remote and N clones
    remoteDir := setupBareRepo(t, tmpDir)
    cloneDirs := make(map[string]string)
    
    for i := 0; i < numClones; i++ {
        name := string(rune('A' + i))
        cloneDirs[name] = setupClone(t, tmpDir, remoteDir, name)
    }
    
    // Each clone creates issue with same ID but different content
    for name, dir := range cloneDirs {
        createIssue(t, dir, fmt.Sprintf("Issue from clone %s", name))
    }
    
    // Sync in specified order
    for _, name := range syncOrder {
        syncClone(t, cloneDirs[name], name)
    }
    
    // Final pull for convergence
    for name, dir := range cloneDirs {
        finalPull(t, dir, name)
    }
    
    // Verify all clones have all N issues
    expectedTitles := make(map[string]bool)
    for i := 0; i < numClones; i++ {
        name := string(rune('A' + i))
        expectedTitles[fmt.Sprintf("Issue from clone %s", name)] = true
    }
    
    for name, dir := range cloneDirs {
        titles := getTitles(t, dir)
        if !compareTitleSets(titles, expectedTitles) {
            t.Errorf("Clone %s missing issues: expected %v, got %v", 
                name, expectedTitles, titles)
        }
    }
    
    t.Log("✓ All", numClones, "clones converged successfully")
}
```

### 3. Add performance benchmarks
Test convergence time and memory usage:

```go
func BenchmarkNWayCollision(b *testing.B) {
    for _, n := range []int{3, 5, 10, 20} {
        b.Run(fmt.Sprintf("N=%d", n), func(b *testing.B) {
            for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ {
                // Run N-way collision and measure time
                testNCloneCollisionBench(b, n)
            }
        })
    }
}
```

### 4. Add convergence time tests
Verify bounded convergence:

```go
func TestConvergenceTime(t *testing.T) {
    // Test that convergence happens within expected rounds
    // For N clones, should converge in at most N-1 sync rounds
    
    for n := 3; n <= 10; n++ {
        t.Run(fmt.Sprintf("N=%d", n), func(t *testing.T) {
            rounds := measureConvergenceRounds(t, n)
            maxExpected := n - 1
            if rounds > maxExpected {
                t.Errorf("Convergence took %d rounds, expected ≤ %d", 
                    rounds, maxExpected)
            }
        })
    }
}
```

### 5. Add edge case tests
Test boundary conditions:
- All N clones have identical content (dedup works)
- N-1 clones have same content, 1 differs
- All N clones have unique content
- Mix of collisions and non-collisions

## Acceptance Criteria
- TestFiveCloneCollision passes with all sync orders
- All 5 clones converge to identical content
- Performance is acceptable (< 5 seconds for 5 clones)
- Convergence time is bounded (≤ N-1 rounds)
- Edge cases handled correctly
- Benchmarks show scalability to 10+ clones

## Files to Create/Modify
- beads_twoclone_test.go or beads_nway_test.go
- Add helper functions for N-clone setup

## Testing Strategy

### Test Matrix
| N Clones | Sync Orders | Expected Result |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| 3        | A→B→C       | Pass            |
| 3        | C→B→A       | Pass            |
| 5        | A→B→C→D→E   | Pass            |
| 5        | E→D→C→B→A   | Pass            |
| 5        | Random      | Pass            |
| 10       | Sequential  | Pass            |

### Performance Targets
- 3 clones: < 2 seconds
- 5 clones: < 5 seconds
- 10 clones: < 15 seconds

## Dependencies
- Requires bd-cbed9619.5, bd-cbed9619.4, bd-cbed9619.3, bd-dcd6f14b to be completed
- TestThreeCloneCollision must pass first

## Success Metrics
- All tests pass for N ∈ {3, 5, 10}
- Convergence time scales linearly (O(N))
- Memory usage reasonable (< 100MB for 10 clones)
- No data corruption or loss in any scenario","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-10-29T19:52:05.462747-07:00","updated_at":"2025-10-31T12:00:43.198413-07:00","closed_at":"2025-10-31T12:00:43.198413-07:00"}

- bd-4t7: auto-import runs during --no-auto-import

Unknown - discovered during bd sync session on 2025-11-26

 {"id":"bd-kzxd","title":"Sync protection mechanism resurrects deleted issues","description":"During bd sync, the 'Protecting N issue(s) from left snapshot' logic resurrects issues that are in the deletions manifest, causing UNIQUE constraint failures on subsequent syncs.

## Reproduction
1. Delete an issue with bd delete (creates tombstone + deletions.jsonl entry)
2. Run bd sync - succeeds
3. Run bd sync again - fails with 'UNIQUE constraint failed: issues.id'

## Root Cause
The protection mechanism (designed to prevent data loss during 3-way merge) keeps a snapshot of issues before sync. When importing after pull, it restores issues from this snapshot even if they're in the deletions manifest.

## Observed Behavior
- bd-3pd was deleted and added to deletions.jsonl
- First sync exports tombstone, commits, pulls, imports - succeeds
- Second sync: protection restores bd-3pd from left snapshot
- Import tries to create bd-3pd which already exists → UNIQUE constraint error

## Expected Behavior
Issues in deletions manifest should NOT be restored by protection mechanism.

## Workaround
Manually delete from DB: sqlite3 .beads/beads.db 'DELETE FROM issues WHERE id = "bd-xxx"'

## Files to Investigate
- cmd/bd/sync.go - protection logic
- cmd/bd/snapshot_manager.go - left snapshot handling
- internal/importer/importer.go - import with protection","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-13T10:11:02.550663-08:00","updated_at":"2025-12-13T10:20:51.651662-08:00","closed_at":"2025-12-13T10:20:51.651662-08:00"}

 {"id":"bd-9ae788be","title":"Implement clone-scoped ID allocation to prevent N-way collisions","description":"## Problem
Current ID allocation uses per-clone atomic counters (issue_counters table) that sync based on local database state. In N-way collision scenarios:
- Clone B sees {test-1} locally, allocates test-2
- Clone D sees {test-1, test-2, test-3} locally, allocates test-4
- When same content gets assigned test-2 and test-4, convergence fails

Root cause: Each clone independently allocates IDs without global coordination, leading to overlapping assignments for the same content.

## Solution
Add clone UUID to ID allocation to make every ID globally unique:

**Current format:** `test-1`, `test-2`, `test-3`
**New format:** `test-1-a7b3`, `test-2-a7b3`, `test-3-c4d9`

Where suffix is first 4 chars of clone UUID.

## Implementation

### 1. Add clone_identity table
```sql
CREATE TABLE clone_identity (
    clone_uuid TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    created_at DATETIME DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
```

### 2. Modify getNextIDForPrefix()
```go
func (s *SQLiteStorage) getNextIDForPrefix(ctx context.Context, prefix string) (string, error) {
    cloneUUID := s.getOrCreateCloneUUID(ctx)
    shortUUID := cloneUUID[:4]
    
    nextNum := s.getNextCounterForPrefix(ctx, prefix)
    return fmt.Sprintf("%s-%d-%s", prefix, nextNum, shortUUID), nil
}
```

### 3. Update ID parsing logic
All places that parse IDs (utils.ExtractIssueNumber, etc.) need to handle new format.

### 4. Migration strategy
- Existing IDs remain unchanged (no suffix)
- New IDs get clone suffix automatically
- Display layer can hide suffix in UI: `bd-cb64c226.3-a7b3` → `#42`

## Benefits
- **Zero collision risk**: Same content in different clones gets different IDs

 {"id":"bd-khnb","title":"bd migrate --update-repo-id triggers auto-import that resurrects deleted issues","description":"**Bug:** Running `bd migrate --update-repo-id` can resurrect previously deleted issues from git history.

## What Happened

User deleted 490 closed issues:
- Deletion committed successfully (06d655a) with JSONL at 48 lines
- Database had 48 issues after deletion
- User ran `bd migrate --update-repo-id` to fix legacy database
- Migration triggered daemon auto-import
- JSONL had been restored to 538 issues (from commit 6cd3a32 - before deletion)
- Auto-import loaded the old JSONL over the cleaned database
- Result: 490 deleted issues resurrected

## Root Cause

The auto-import logic in `cmd/bd/sync.go:130-136`:
```go
if isJSONLNewer(jsonlPath) {
    fmt.Println("→ JSONL is newer than database, importing first...")
    if err := importFromJSONL(ctx, jsonlPath, renameOnImport); err != nil {
```

This checks if JSONL mtime is newer than database and auto-imports. The problem:
1. Git operations (pull, merge, checkout) can restore old JSONL files
2. Restored file has recent mtime (time of git operation)
3. Auto-import sees "newer" JSONL and imports it
4. Old data overwrites current database state

## Timeline

- 19:59: Commit 6cd3a32 restored JSONL to 538 issues from d99222d
- 20:22: Commit 3520321 (bd sync)
- 20:23: Commit 06d655a deleted 490 issues → JSONL now 48 lines
- 20:23: User ran `bd migrate --update-repo-id`
- Migration completed, daemon started
- Daemon saw JSONL (restored earlier to 538) was "newer" than database
- Auto-import resurrected 490 deleted issues

## Impact

- **Critical data loss bug** - user deletions can be undone silently
- Affects any workflow that uses git branches, merges, or checkouts
- Auto-import has no safety checks against importing older data
- Users have no warning that old data will overwrite current state

## Fix Options

1. **Content-based staleness** (not mtime-based)
   - Compare JSONL content hash vs database content hash
   - Only import if content actually changed
   - Prevents re-importing old data with new mtime

2. **Database timestamp check**
   - Store "last export timestamp" in database metadata
   - Only import JSONL if it's newer than last export
   - Prevents importing old JSONL after git operations

3. **User confirmation**
   - Before auto-import, show diff of what will change
   - Require confirmation for large changes (>10% issues affected)
   - Safety valve for destructive imports

4. **Explicit sync mode**
   - Disable auto-import entirely
   - Require explicit `bd sync` or `bd import` commands
   - Trade convenience for safety

## Recommended Solution

Combination of #1 and #2:
- Add `last_export_timestamp` to database metadata
- Check JSONL mtime > last_export_timestamp before importing
- Add content hash check as additional safety
- Show warning if importing would delete >10 issues

This preserves auto-import convenience while preventing data loss.

## Files Involved

- `cmd/bd/sync.go:130-136` - Auto-import logic
- `cmd/bd/daemon_sync.go` - Daemon export/import cycle
- `internal/autoimport/autoimport.go` - Staleness detection

## Reproduction Steps

1. Create and delete some issues, commit to git
2. Checkout an earlier commit (before deletion)
3. Checkout back to current commit
4. JSONL file now has recent mtime but old content
5. Run any bd command that triggers auto-import
6. Deleted issues are resurrected","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-11-20T20:44:35.235807-05:00","updated_at":"2025-11-20T21:51:31.806158-05:00","closed_at":"2025-11-20T21:51:31.806158-05:00"}

## Current Behavior
`main.go:561` always creates FlushManager:
```go
flushManager = NewFlushManager(autoFlushEnabled, getDebounceDuration())
```

Even with `--sandbox` (which sets `noAutoFlush=true`), the FlushManager goroutine is still running.

## Proposed Change
```go
// Only create FlushManager if we might actually need it
if !sandboxMode && autoFlushEnabled {
    flushManager = NewFlushManager(true, getDebounceDuration())
}
```

## Handle nil FlushManager
Update PersistentPostRun to handle nil:
```go
if flushManager != nil && !skipFinalFlush {
    if err := flushManager.Shutdown(); err != nil {
        // ...
    }
}
```

## Synchronous Export Fallback
When sandbox mode + write operation occurs, do synchronous export:
```go
if sandboxMode && isDirty {
    performSynchronousExport()
}
```

## Also: Default lock-timeout to 100ms in sandbox mode
In PersistentPreRun, when sandboxMode is detected:
```go
if sandboxMode {
    noDaemon = true
    noAutoFlush = true
    noAutoImport = true
    if lockTimeout == 30*time.Second { // Not explicitly set
        lockTimeout = 100 * time.Millisecond
    }
}
```

```go
func (s *SQLiteStorage) CreateIssue(ctx context.Context, issue *types.Issue) error {
    // Generate hash ID if not provided

        issue.ID = idgen.GenerateHashID(
            issue.Title,
            issue.Description,
            time.Now(),
            s.workspaceID,
        )
    }
    
    // Assign next alias

    // Insert with hash ID + alias

}
```

 {"id":"bd-zpnq","title":"Daemons don't exit when parent process dies, causing accumulation and race conditions","description":"Multiple daemon processes accumulate over time because daemons don't automatically stop when their parent process (e.g., coding agent) is killed. This causes:

1. Race conditions: 8+ daemons watching same .beads/beads.db, each with own 30s debounce timer
2. Git conflicts: Multiple daemons racing to commit/push .beads/issues.jsonl
3. Resource waste: Orphaned daemons from sessions days/hours old still running

Example: User had 8 daemons from multiple sessions (12:37AM, 7:20PM, 7:22PM, 7:47PM, 9:19PM yesterday + 9:54AM, 10:55AM today).

Solutions to consider:
1. Track parent PID and exit when parent dies
2. Use single global daemon instead of per-session
3. Document manual cleanup: pkill -f "bd daemon"
4. Add daemon lifecycle management (auto-cleanup of stale daemons)","notes":"Implementation complete:

1. Added ParentPID field to DaemonLockInfo struct (stored in daemon.lock JSON)
2. Daemon now tracks parent PID via os.Getppid() at startup
3. Both event loops (polling and event-driven) check parent process every 10 seconds
4. Daemon gracefully exits if parent process dies (detected via isProcessRunning check)
5. Handles edge cases:
   - ParentPID=0: Older daemons without tracking (ignored)

 {"id":"bd-iq7n","title":"Audit and fix JSONL filename mismatches across all repo clones","description":"## Problem

Multiple clones of repos are configured with different JSONL filenames (issues.jsonl vs beads.jsonl), causing:
1. JSONL files to be resurrected after deletion (one clone pushes issues.jsonl, another pushes beads.jsonl)
2. Agents unable to see issues filed by other agents after sync
3. Merge conflicts and data inconsistencies

## Root Cause

When repos were "bd doctored" or initialized at different times, some got issues.jsonl (old default) and others got beads.jsonl (Beads repo specific). These clones push their respective files, creating duplicates.

## Task

Scan all repo clones under ~/src/ (1-2 levels deep) and standardize their JSONL configuration.

### Step 1: Find all beads-enabled repos

```bash
# Find all directories named 'beads' at levels 1-2 under ~/src/
find ~/src -maxdepth 2 -type d -name beads
```

### Step 2: For each repo found, check configuration

For each directory from Step 1, check:
- Does `.beads/metadata.json` exist?
- What is the `jsonl_export` value?
- What JSONL files actually exist in `.beads/`?
- Are there multiple JSONL files (problem!)?

### Step 3: Create audit report

Generate a report showing:
```
Repo Path                           | Config        | Actual Files           | Status
----------------------------------- | ------------- | ---------------------- | --------
~/src/beads                         | beads.jsonl   | beads.jsonl            | OK
~/src/dave/beads                    | issues.jsonl  | issues.jsonl           | MISMATCH
~/src/emma/beads                    | issues.jsonl  | issues.jsonl, beads.jsonl | DUPLICATE!
```

### Step 4: Determine canonical name for each repo

For repos that are the SAME git repository (check `git remote -v`):
- Group them together
- Determine which JSONL filename should be canonical (majority wins, or beads.jsonl for the beads repo itself)
- List which clones need to be updated

### Step 5: Generate fix script

Create a script that for each mismatched clone:
1. Updates `.beads/metadata.json` to use the canonical name
2. If JSONL file needs renaming: `git mv .beads/old.jsonl .beads/new.jsonl`
3. Removes any duplicate JSONL files: `git rm .beads/duplicate.jsonl`
4. Commits the change
5. Syncs: `bd sync`

### Expected Output

1. Audit report showing all repos and their config status
2. List of repos grouped by git remote (same repository)
3. Fix script or manual instructions for standardizing each repo
4. Verification that after fixes, all clones of the same repo use the same JSONL filename

### Edge Cases

- Handle repos without metadata.json (use default discovery)
- Handle repos with no git remote (standalone/local)
- Handle repos that are not git repositories
- Don't modify repos with uncommitted changes (warn instead)

### Success Criteria

- All clones of the same git repository use the same JSONL filename
- No duplicate JSONL files in any repo
- All configurations documented in metadata.json
- bd doctor passes on all repos","status":"open","issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-11-21T23:58:35.044762-08:00","updated_at":"2025-11-21T23:58:35.044762-08:00"}

 {"id":"bd-mn9p","title":"bd-hv01: Brittle string comparison breaks with JSON field reordering","description":"## Problem
deletion_tracking.go:125 uses string comparison to detect unchanged issues:

```go
if leftLine, existsInLeft := leftIndex[id]; existsInLeft && leftLine == baseLine {
    deletions = append(deletions, id)
}
```

This breaks if:
- JSON field order changes (legal in JSON)
- Timestamps updated by import/export
- Whitespace/formatting changes
- Floating point precision varies

## Example Failure
```json
// baseLine
{"id":"bd-1","priority":1,"status":"open"}
// leftLine (same data, different order)
{"id":"bd-1","status":"open","priority":1}
```
These are semantically identical but string comparison fails.

## Fix
Parse and compare JSON semantically:
```go
func jsonEquals(a, b string) bool {
    var objA, objB map[string]interface{}
    json.Unmarshal([]byte(a), &objA)
    json.Unmarshal([]byte(b), &objB)
    return reflect.DeepEqual(objA, objB)
}
```

- cmd/bd/deletion_tracking.go:125
- cmd/bd/deletion_tracking.go:134-170 (buildIDToLineMap)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-11-06T18:15:35.090716-08:00","updated_at":"2025-11-06T18:46:55.889888-08:00","closed_at":"2025-11-06T18:46:55.889888-08:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-mn9p","depends_on_id":"bd-rbxi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-11-06T18:19:14.790898-08:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"bd-rtp","title":"Implement signal-aware context in CLI commands","description":"Replace context.Background() with signal.NotifyContext() to enable graceful cancellation.

## Context
Part of context propagation work (bd-350). Phase 1 infrastructure is complete - sqlite.New() now accepts context parameter.

## Implementation
Set up signal-aware context at the CLI entry points:

```go
ctx, cancel := signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), os.Interrupt, syscall.SIGTERM)
defer cancel()
```

## Key Locations
- cmd/bd/main.go:438 (marked with TODO(bd-350))
- cmd/bd/daemon.go:317 (marked with TODO(bd-350))
- Other command entry points

## Benefits
- Ctrl+C cancels ongoing database operations
- Graceful shutdown on SIGTERM
- Better user experience during long operations

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Ctrl+C during import cancels operation cleanly
- [ ] Ctrl+C during export cancels operation cleanly
- [ ] No database corruption on cancellation
- [ ] Proper cleanup on signal (defers execute)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-11-20T21:26:34.621983-05:00","updated_at":"2025-11-20T21:32:26.288303-05:00","closed_at":"2025-11-20T21:32:26.288303-05:00"}
